findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is the presence of multiple intra-articular loose bodies of relatively uniform size, which may demonstrate 'rings and arcs' calcification [1, 2]. There are associated well-marginated mechanical erosions of the adjacent articular bone surfaces [1, 2].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include widening of the joint space due to the accumulation of these loose bodies [2]. The adjacent bone mineralization is preserved, and there may be pressure scalloping of the bone in joints with tight capsules [2].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of diffuse periarticular osteopenia or uniform joint space loss [2, 3]. Furthermore, there is no 'blooming' artifact on gradient-echo imaging to suggest hemosiderin deposition [4, 5].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of multiple, uniform-sized calcified intra-articular bodies with mechanical erosions and normal background bone density [1, 2].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Synovial (osteo)chondromatosis, given the characteristic multiple calcified loose bodies and well-marginated mechanical erosions [1, 2]. This condition is a benign synovial metaplasia that typically presents in large joints such as the knee, elbow, shoulder, or hip [1].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS), however this typically presents with a large joint effusion and low T1 and T2 signal synovial nodules that bloom on gradient-echo sequences due to hemosiderin [1, 4]. Furthermore, calcification is very rare in PVNS, whereas it is present in approximately 85% of synovial chondromatosis cases [1, 6].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Rheumatoid arthritis (RA), given that it can also present with erosions and 'rice bodies' which can mimic loose bodies [7, 8]. Missing active RA could delay appropriate disease-modifying therapy, but RA is typically polyarticular, bilateral, and demonstrates periarticular osteopenia and uniform joint space loss, which are absent here [2, 3].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the extent of the loose bodies and evaluate for any secondary severe degenerative osteoarthritis or malignant transformation to synovial chondrosarcoma [1, 9].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to assess the rate of loose body accumulation and to monitor for progressive mechanical cartilage and bone destruction [2, 9].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the affected joint, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the unmineralized chondral loose bodies, which would demonstrate high T2 signal, and for defining the extent of synovial hypertrophy and chondral defects [5].
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1-weighted, fluid-sensitive (T2/STIR), and gradient-echo (GRE) sequences to characterize the loose bodies, differentiate them from hemosiderin-laden PVNS (which would bloom on GRE), and assess the articular cartilage integrity [5, 10].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Orthopaedic MDT. My report would clearly document the number and location of the loose bodies, the presence and depth of bony erosions, and the integrity of the articular cartilage to guide surgical synovectomy and loose body removal [1, 9].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is the presence of multiple intra-articular loose bodies of relatively uniform size, which may demonstrate 'rings and arcs' calcification [1, 2]. There are associated well-marginated mechanical erosions of the adjacent articular bone surfaces [1, 2]. Supporting features include widening of the joint space due to the accumulation of these loose bodies [2]. The adjacent bone mineralization is preserved, and there may be pressure scalloping of the bone in joints with tight capsules [2]. Importantly, there is no evidence of diffuse periarticular osteopenia or uniform joint space loss [2, 3]. Furthermore, there is no 'blooming' artifact on gradient-echo imaging to suggest hemosiderin deposition [4, 5]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of multiple, uniform-sized calcified intra-articular bodies with mechanical erosions and normal background bone density [1, 2].

The appearances are most in keeping with Synovial (osteo)chondromatosis, given the characteristic multiple calcified loose bodies and well-marginated mechanical erosions [1, 2]. This condition is a benign synovial metaplasia that typically presents in large joints such as the knee, elbow, shoulder, or hip [1]. The principle differential would be Pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS), however this typically presents with a large joint effusion and low T1 and T2 signal synovial nodules that bloom on gradient-echo sequences due to hemosiderin [1, 4]. Furthermore, calcification is very rare in PVNS, whereas it is present in approximately 85% of synovial chondromatosis cases [1, 6]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Rheumatoid arthritis (RA), given that it can also present with erosions and 'rice bodies' which can mimic loose bodies [7, 8]. Missing active RA could delay appropriate disease-modifying therapy, but RA is typically polyarticular, bilateral, and demonstrates periarticular osteopenia and uniform joint space loss, which are absent here [2, 3].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the extent of the loose bodies and evaluate for any secondary severe degenerative osteoarthritis or malignant transformation to synovial chondrosarcoma [1, 9]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to assess the rate of loose body accumulation and to monitor for progressive mechanical cartilage and bone destruction [2, 9]. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the affected joint, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the unmineralized chondral loose bodies, which would demonstrate high T2 signal, and for defining the extent of synovial hypertrophy and chondral defects [5]. I would specify T1-weighted, fluid-sensitive (T2/STIR), and gradient-echo (GRE) sequences to characterize the loose bodies, differentiate them from hemosiderin-laden PVNS (which would bloom on GRE), and assess the articular cartilage integrity [5, 10]. This should go to the Orthopaedic MDT. My report would clearly document the number and location of the loose bodies, the presence and depth of bony erosions, and the integrity of the articular cartilage to guide surgical synovectomy and loose body removal [1, 9].
